subtitle: When the Vijayanagara Lion Took Up the Poet’s Quill

panels.action: Krishnadevaraya stands on a palace terrace of Vijayanagara, one hand on his sword hilt and the other holding a palm-leaf manuscript, the Tungabhadra river and temple towers stretching into a hazy golden horizon.

dialogue.text: In the high age of Vijayanagara, Krishnadevaraya, lion of war, took up the poet’s quill to write Amuktamalyada and bind sword, temple, and song into one radiant dharma.

i18n.hi: विजयनगर के उत्कर्ष युग में युद्धसिंह कृष्णदेवराय ने अमुक्तमाल्यदा लिखने को कलम उठाई, और तलवार, मंदिर और गीत को एक उज्ज्वल धर्म में बाँध दिया।

panels.action: From above, Krishnadevaraya rides in armor through Vijayanagara’s bustling streets, flanked by soldiers and banners, as traders, scholars, and pilgrims throng around towering gopurams and market stalls.

dialogue.text: He ruled between war and worship: one eye on marching armies, the other on rising temple towers, guarding a world where markets, monasteries, and mandapams shared the same dust.

i18n.hi: वह युद्ध और पूजा के बीच राज करता था; एक नज़र सेनाओं पर, दूसरी मंदिर शिखरों पर, उस जग को बचाते हुए जहाँ बाज़ार, मठ और मंडप एक ही धूल में साँस लेते थे।

panels.shot: slow push-in from wide court to medium on throne
panels.action: In a grand audience hall lined with carved pillars, Krishnadevaraya sits on a high throne, courtiers and foreign envoys below, while scribes stand to one side with palm-leaf bundles and ink.

dialogue.text: Vijayanagara’s court was not only the seat of tribute and treaty. It was a workshop of words, where palm leaves carried royal orders, edicts of land grants, and verses that braided power with piety.

i18n.hi: विजयनगर का दरबार सिर्फ़ कर और संधि की गादी नहीं था; वह शब्दों की कार्यशाला था, जहाँ ताड़पत्रों पर फरमान, भूदान के लेख और भक्ति से जुड़ी सत्ता की कविताएँ चलती थीं।

panels.shot: medium close-up on contemplative face at window
panels.action: Krishnadevaraya, now in simple royal robes, stands by a lattice window at dusk, looking out at a temple gopuram as a priest’s conch sounds faintly in the distance.

dialogue.text: Scholars remember him as conqueror of lands. Yet manuscripts whisper another title: Krishnadevaraya the author, whose thoughts turned from fort walls to the inner walls of the heart.

i18n.hi: विद्वान उसे विजेता कहते हैं, पर पांडुलिपियाँ फुसफुसाती हैं दूसरा नाम: लेखक कृष्णदेवराय, जिसकी सोच किले की दीवारों से मन की दीवारों की ओर मुड़ी।

panels.action: Inside a quiet palace chamber lit by oil lamps, Krishnadevaraya sits cross-legged before a low desk, palm leaf and stylus in hand, a small bronze image of Vishnu on a side table.

dialogue.text: Telugu scribes copied a bold claim on their leaves: that the king himself had composed a kavya called Amuktamalyada, weaving governance into a tale of devotion.

i18n.hi: तेलुगु लेखकों ने ताड़पत्रों पर साहसी दावा लिखा: कि स्वयं राजा ने अमुक्तमाल्यदा नाम का काव्य रचा, राजकाज को भक्ति कथा में बुनते हुए।

panels.shot: gentle low-angle on Krishnadevaraya before a Tamil shrine
panels.action: The king, dressed as a pilgrim, stands barefoot before a shrine with Tamil inscriptions, lamps glowing, as priests and common pilgrims cluster in reverent silence.

dialogue.text: His imagination reached south, toward Srirangam and the songs of Tamil Alvars. In that meeting of tongues, he found a story that could carry the weight of a king’s dharma.

i18n.hi: उसकी कल्पना दक्षिण की ओर श्रीरंगम और तमिल आल्वारों के गीतों तक पहुँची; भाषाओं की उस मुलाक़ात में उसे एक ऐसी कथा मिली जो राजा के धर्म का भार उठा सके।

panels.shot: extreme close-up on stylus touching palm leaf
panels.action: A close view of Krishnadevaraya’s hand as he carefully inscribes Telugu letters onto a palm leaf, the texture of the leaf and the sharp stylus tip clearly visible.

dialogue.text: He chose the tale of Andal, the girl who offered garlands to Ranganatha before her own wear, and dared to retell it in Telugu, bridging Tamil bhakti and the Vijayanagara court’s language.

i18n.hi: उसने आंडाल की कथा चुनी—वह लड़की जो पहले विष्णु रंगनाथ को ही माला चढ़ाती थी—और उसे तेलुगु में फिर से कहने का साहस किया, तमिल भक्ति को विजयनगर दरबार की भाषा से जोड़ते हुए।

panels.shot: slow push-in on focused face lit by lamp
panels.action: Krishnadevaraya leans over his work, face lit by a single lamp, eyes intense yet serene, shadows of scripts and book bundles surrounding him.

dialogue.text: Amuktamalyada was more than a sweet story. In Andal’s questions to her god, he slipped in questions of rule: how should a lord care for his people, as a deity cares for devotees?

i18n.hi: अमुक्तमाल्यदा सिर्फ़ मधुर कहानी नहीं थी; आंडाल के अपने भगवान से सवालों में उसने राज के सवाल टाँके—एक स्वामी अपनी प्रजा का ख्याल कैसे रखे, जैसे देवता भक्तों का रखते हैं?

panels.shot: medium shot, king reading aloud to two unseen listeners off-frame
panels.action: The king sits upright, reciting from a palm leaf, with two shadowy figures of scholars half-visible beyond a carved pillar, listening intently.

dialogue.speaker: krishnadevaraya
dialogue.text: A ruler must know hunger and hope in his people’s eyes.

i18n.hi: राजा को अपनी प्रजा की आँखों में भूख और उम्मीद दोनों पहचाननी चाहिए।

panels.shot: wide interior court shot with poets in loose group
panels.action: In a side hall of the palace, Krishnadevaraya gestures warmly toward a small gathering of learned men in varied dress, seated on carpets with manuscripts beside them.

dialogue.text: Around him gathered masters of Telugu verse—Allasani Peddana, Nandi Thimmana, Dhurjati, Pingali Surana, and others—each with his own cadence, not yet bound into any formal ‘eight’.

i18n.hi: उसके चारों ओर तेलुगु काव्य के महारथी जुटे—अल्लसानी पेद्दन, नंदी तिम्मन, धूर्जटी, पिंगली सुरन और कई अन्य—हर एक अपनी अलग लय में, अभी तक किसी तय ‘आठ’ में बाँधे बिना।

panels.shot: medium shot, side angle on king among poets
panels.action: Krishnadevaraya sits slightly off-center among the poets, not on a throne but on a low seat, listening as one elderly poet gestures while speaking, others nodding.

dialogue.text: Later memory would crown them as Ashtadiggajas, the eight elephants of the directions. In their own day, they were a living circle of patron and poets, reshaping Telugu for empire and devotion.

i18n.hi: बाद की यादें उन्हें अष्टदिग्गज, दिशाओं के आठ गजराज नाम देंगी; उनके समय में वे राजा और कवियों का जीवंत गोला थे, जो साम्राज्य और भक्ति के लिए तेलुगु को नया रूप दे रहे थे।

panels.shot: medium close-up on king presenting manuscript to a poet
panels.action: The king extends a neatly tied bundle of palm leaves to an older poet seated before him, the poet’s eyes wide with respect and surprise.

dialogue.text: Krishnadevaraya was not content to fund other men’s words. He placed his own manuscript among theirs, letting the line between king and poet blur in the glow of shared craft.

i18n.hi: कृष्णदेवराय सिर्फ़ दूसरों के शब्दों को धन देने से संतुष्ट नहीं था; उसने अपना ग्रंथ भी उनके बीच रख दिया, ताकि राजा और कवि की रेखा साझा साधना की रोशनी में धुंधली हो जाए।

panels.shot: low-angle shot of recital in mandapam
panels.action: In a temple mandapam, the king sits respectfully to one side as a court reciter chants verses from Amuktamalyada to an attentive mix of priests, merchants, and soldiers.

dialogue.text: Verses of Amuktamalyada traveled beyond palace walls, chanted in mandapams where villagers and priests heard in Andal’s passion a king’s promise to rule with care.

i18n.hi: अमुक्तमाल्यदा के श्लोक महल की दीवारों से बाहर निकले, मंडपों में गूँजे, जहाँ गाँव वाले और पुरोहित, आंडाल की तड़प में राजा की सौम्य शासन-प्रतिज्ञा सुनते थे।

panels.shot: split-depth shot: foreground poet writing, background king reviewing papers
panels.action: In the same hall, a poet in the foreground writes on palm leaves, while in the background Krishnadevaraya studies a stack of documents and land grants beside a temple diagram.

dialogue.text: As scholars later showed, Vijayanagara’s patronage of literature was part of its politics. Poems, like tax records and temple grants, were instruments to imagine and display just rule.

i18n.hi: विद्वानों ने बाद में दिखाया कि विजयनगर की साहित्य-सेवा उसकी राजनीति का हिस्सा थी; कविता भी कर की सूचियों और मंदिर भूदानों की तरह न्यायपूर्ण राज की कल्पना और प्रदर्शन का औज़ार थी।

panels.shot: medium shot by the Tungabhadra, king in reflective mood
panels.action: On a riverbank near the Tungabhadra, Krishnadevaraya stands with a single companion holding manuscripts, watching boats glide past between temple-dotted hills.

dialogue.text: From the river that fed his capital, his words flowed out in two streams: the steel of campaigns, recorded in inscriptions, and the softness of poetry, recorded in palm leaves.

i18n.hi: राजधानी को सींचने वाली नदी से उसकी बातें दो धाराओं में बहीं: अभियानों की इस्पात, जो शिलालेखों में दर्ज हुई, और कविता की कोमलता, जो ताड़पत्रों पर उतरी।

panels.shot: dramatic interior shot, lamp-lit manuscripts being stored
panels.action: Inside a dim archive room, attendants carefully stack bundles of palm-leaf manuscripts, one labeled distinctly, while a distant silhouette of Krishnadevaraya watches from the doorway.

dialogue.text: Manuscripts bearing his name survived centuries of heat, insects, and shifting power. Their script testifies that the king’s authorship was no idle legend but a claim preserved in inked leaves.

i18n.hi: उसके नाम वाली पांडुलिपियाँ सदियों की गर्मी, कीड़ों और बदलती सत्ता से बच निकलीं; उनकी लिखावट गवाही देती है कि राजा का लेखक होना कोई हवा में उड़ा किस्सा नहीं, बल्कि स्याही में बचा दावा है।

panels.shot: time-lapse style wide shot blending old palm leaves with modern printed book
panels.action: Palm leaves fade into a modern printed book of Amuktamalyada resting on a table, as ghostly silhouettes of king and poets stand faintly behind, overlooking the volume.

dialogue.text: In our time, new translators like Rao and Shulman have carried Amuktamalyada into print, letting fresh eyes see how a sixteenth-century king thought through love, language, and rule.

i18n.hi: आज हमारे समय में राव और शुलमैन जैसे नए अनुवादकों ने अमुक्तमाल्यदा को मुद्रित जग में ला दिया है, ताकि नई आँखें देखें कि सोलहवीं सदी का राजा प्रेम, भाषा और राज को कैसे सोचता था।

panels.shot: extreme close-up on printed words "Krishnadevaraya" and "Amuktamalyada"
panels.action: A close view of the title page of a modern edition of Amuktamalyada, with Krishnadevaraya’s name prominently displayed, soft light highlighting the text.

dialogue.text: History now sees Krishnadevaraya not only as the apex of Vijayanagara’s military strength, but as a ruler who understood that the finest crowns rest on both sword and song.

i18n.hi: अब इतिहास कृष्णदेवराय को केवल विजयनगर की सैन्य शक्ति की चोटी नहीं, बल्कि ऐसे शासक के रूप में देखता है जो समझ गया था कि सर्वोत्तम मुकुट तलवार और गीत दोनों पर टिकते हैं।

subtitle_i18n.en: When the Vijayanagara Lion Took Up the Poet’s Quill
subtitle_i18n.hi: जब विजयनगर का सिंह कवि की कलम उठा
